speaker: interviewer
text: This is Lynn [? Weingarten ?] interviewing Sam Carmel, Los Angeles, California, June 21, 1995, tape three.

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't go nowhere without the baby. And she had a, a, a, a carriage. And she was always in that-- stroller, I mean. And she was sleeping there as a little one. And then we went to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Czechoslovakia. And from Czechoslovakia, we were there a couple days. We went to Austria. [? We was in ?] Vienna, Austria. And, and we see that we cannot, we cannot remain in Austria, because we don't have no room. And we wouldn't want to sit there?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you sleep when you go with a baby and your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in the committee. In the committee there, we were sleeping. But then we went to Germany. In Germany, Babenhausen was a-- from material made-- [INAUDIBLE]. And-- but they have-- where you can sleep in there. That's [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you in Germany? What's the name of the city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Babenhausen. Then we-- they took some of them to Reichenbach, some of them to other cities. But we went to Reichenbach. And there, we got a-- there, we got already our apartment. They give a-- gave us an apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who gave it to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The committee, the Jewish committee gave us an apartment. Because I had a family. So I was living in that rooms. And then they were coming from Russia, a lot of refugees. But they went to Russia. And they came back to Poland. But every train, I went to look for her parents, her father, her sister, is they on the trains? But [? they came again. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: She went with you to look?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she had to be with the baby. So I, I, I went by myself. And I was asking-- I didn't know how it looked like. But I, I was asking for his name till so long, so many days, till I finally found him. He came--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Szika, like my name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: And last name-- Wojslowitzr is his last name. And I found him. And they got from Russia there the-- all kind of pots, and pans, and [INAUDIBLE], and some food, everything. And I took those pots and pans. I said, leave it. Somebody else may need it. You could--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find him? You knew his name? You [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went from one, one wagon to the other-- one after the other, one after the other. And I was calling his name till finally, finally, I found him. And then it took me a few days. But I found him. And then he arrived. He came also with a child and a wife. And, and the sister wasn't with him. Her sister wasn't with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was he with?

speaker: interviewee
text: With a wife, a new wife. He got married, because he found out that his wife was killed. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: A new child?

speaker: interviewee
text: A new child.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your wife had a new brother or sister she never knew?

speaker: interviewee
text: She never knew. And listen to this. So I took away those pots and pans. I said, leave it here. Somebody going to need and they're going to pick up and have it. You wouldn't need it. You can have everything what I have here It's going to be yours. And I gave him everything. And I gave him-- I found myself another apartment. And he got my apartment with everything what I had. And he was living there. And then finally, I got in touch with my cousins in America-- not I wasn't get in touch, because I didn't have the address. But the, the, the organization was [? in Poland, ?] [INAUDIBLE]. They, they, they got in touch with my cousin that I'm alive. And they was so-- they didn't know what to do for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your cousin's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was nine brothers and one sister, Carmel brothers from [INAUDIBLE] in New York. They were my cousin, first cousin. Their father and my father were brothers. My father helped them go to America, the oldest one. And he broughted down the brothers with the mother. The father couldn't go no more. He was very sick. And he died in Poland. Anyway, when I-- I got in touch with them. They tried to guarantee for me that I should come to America. They going to give me everything what, what I will need.

speaker: interviewee
text: They guaranteed for me that I go-- not going to be on the government-- the government should support me. They would support me, that they-- how they calling it? They, they going to finance me everything. And sure enough, finally, I arrived in-- to New York. When was that, Sam?

speaker: interviewee
text: In March the-- March the 14th, 1949, 1949.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who came with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife and my daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father-in-law stayed?

speaker: interviewee
text: He remained in my apartment. I couldn't take him with me, because I cannot guarantee I can feed him or something. You understand? Here, he was already.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the years that you lived in this camp was two years? How long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: But more than two years. 1946-- 194--

speaker: interviewee
text: Three years I was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you earned money by black market all along, by selling?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was, I was doing anything I can so my--

speaker: interviewer
text: Like what? Like what?

speaker: interviewee
text: To supply my family with food, with everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was going to [INAUDIBLE] in the, in the, in the village. And from there, I bought for them whatever I could. And I sold it to our people what they need, what they wanted.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your wife? What was your wife doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife took care of the house and the, and the baby.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you got married?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got married in Germany, before I went to America, because--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was at the wedding?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were friends at the wedding, and no family were in it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father-in-law?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father wasn't here. He was in Germany. No, this I was-- my father-in-law remained in-- what was it? In Reichenbach. Then I went-- I went to--

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]. Yeah, I was in, in--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so now you're to the part where you and your wife are going to come to America. It's March, 1949. Tell--

speaker: interviewee
text: I arrived in America.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like for you, when you got-- tell us.

speaker: interviewee
text: I always dreamed that I ever going to be in America. My whole life, when I was little, a little boy, I was dr-- always thinking and hoping that I will go to America someday. I will be in America. And this was the moment when I arrived in America.

speaker: interviewee
text: My cousins and aunt, everybody was there by the ship where I came off the ship. And they were-- they didn't know that to [? do ?] [? with ?] me. It was, was-- they search like they would find God, you know? It's so much they, they admired me that I have arrived. I have a daughter.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my daughter was a beauty. I have her in the pictures. And, and they, they did everything for me. First, I was staying with my, our cousin. But those of us from Europe, we came right before the war. I was staying with him. Then they gave me an apartment. The cousins, they gave me apartment. They bought me furniture. They gave me money to live on. They took me up to their factory where they had the factory. To them, anybody would want to come to work had to be sent from the union. He couldn't hire anybody without the union. And he took me over without telling the union out. And [INAUDIBLE]. I'm his, his flesh and blood, you know, His cousin, only cousin remain alive. And they did so much for me. They put me in there on a chair to sit, and to learn how to sew on the machine. They want-- they said, whatever you like, we're going to teach you. You're going to have a job for your life. Never mind the union. We going to settle with the union. If they going to come, they going to bother with us. Don't worry about it. This our worry, our problem. Anyway, and they did it more than they could. But I couldn't sit on the chair, because my back was hurting. I never were sitting by my work. I was never sitting, always standing I was working.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I wasn't happy. So they says to me, what is wrong? Is any problem with your wife, or-- tell us. We are here to help you. We don't want you to be unhappy. We want you to be happy. So I said to them, my teeth hurts me. They send me to their doctor-- their dentist to fix my teeth. Still, I'm not happy. So they sent another cousin, [INAUDIBLE]. He was from [? four ?] business, [? in four ?] business, [? always ?] our cousin. His wife was my cousin. And he came and took me out to lunch. And he start talking with me. What is wrong? Why aren't you happy? We doing everything for you. What else can you expect?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said that-- said to him, you take a bird from the street. You giving them warm place. You giving them water. And you giving them food.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, still, the bird doesn't like it, because he wants to be free and, and outside. So they doing for me-- how can I say to them, I'm not happy, because they giving everything? How can I tell them that I am not happy with that?

speaker: interviewee
text: But I'm going to be happy because I cannot sit. My back hurts me when I'm sitting so many hours. And I don't want to tell them that it hurts me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And [INAUDIBLE] want to do? I said, I would like to own business. A business? What kind of business you like?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, where, where I was working in Krakow. He said, oh, forget about that business. This business is very-- you have to be a millionaire to go in that business.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of business was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Textile-- that kind, you have to be am millionaire. You giving on trust. And then after the season, they don't pay you. They giving you back the, the merchandise. And then it goes out of printer style. And he explained to me what is wrong-- what I cannot have it. He gave me the suggestion as to go in, in grocery business. OK, you have to know the grocery business. You cannot just open a store without knowing, he said. He said, I have a cousin, an uncle-- his co-- uncle-- from [INAUDIBLE]. He is a-- he can find you a job. I will tell him. And he will find you a job in a grocery. Sure enough, they sent me to a, a supermarket-- a small supermarket. He was very busy. And he saw on me that I'm a businessman. I'm not just a worker. He could tell. And he send me I should watch the, the appetizing place. And I should learn there a little bit. And then I'm going to be on the appe-- appetizing clerk. I don't have to learn for long. I catch up right away. And I start already there in selling whitefish, whatever they was, lox, whatever it is.

speaker: interviewee
text: So a, a, a pound was whitefish was a dol-- $0.99 a pound. So a quarter of a pound is a quarter. I don't know of a lot [? of-- ?] [? other ?] places.

speaker: interviewee
text: He calls me on the side. And he says, you know you have to make up your wages on the, on the customers. You have to like charge them a little bit more. I said, look, I had a different teacher. He taught me whene-- you can make any profit you want to make. But never cheat the customer on the weight or the measure. Always give them the right measure. And always give them the right weight. Because if you charge them more, they want it, they're buying. They don't want it, they don't buy. But if you cheating them on the weight, makes you a crook. And then they wouldn't come to you. They wouldn't want you to wait on them. And you cannot do those things. And I told him that. And I wouldn't work for you. Give me what's coming to me. And I'm quitting on the spot, I'm not going to do that. He paid me. And I quit. I quit because he needs cooks. I am not a crook. Finally, I, he-- I went back to that cousin. I said, I, I quit there because he told me to do this. And I, I refused. And I said, I don't want to work for you no more. And he paid me. And I wanted another job. He find me a job, a private person-- a small store, private person. I worked there a couple months. He didn't pay me much. But they, they paid for my food, for my-- the, the cousins-- for my rooms, for everything. They paid for food. They gave me money. I worked there. Then he had to go in hospital for an operation, the boss. And he says to me, you're going to run the business. I said, I, I am not sure what I-- I'm leaving up to you. I see you know more than you, you think. I can tell. You going to run the business. My wife is going to come to you for money. Write it up how much money you give her. Nobody, nobody, should go to the register. Only you should take care the register. Pay out money what you owe. And write everything written. Write down what you took in every day. Write it down. Everything, I want you black and white. And I did it. And I was running the business. Then comes in the salesman.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he says to me, you know what? I will lend you money and buy this business. And you gonna pay me back after when you going to have the business, when you going to make the money. But now you-- I will lend you the money to buy this, this business.

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell, thank you very much for being so kind to me. But I have cousins. They promised me they're going to give me money to go to open my business. They promised me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I have to learn more about it. And, and how, how can I come to the man? He got sick. And he's in the hospital. Sell me your business.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not expecting he should die. He's going to come back. And he going to be his business.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sam, were you feeling safe in the United States at this time? Did you feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I'm not worrying that I might get killed when I drive from this. I feel safe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Different than you felt ever in your life before?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. Anyway, so I was working. And then I-- we came together with my cousins. They invited me with my wife, with the daughter, the, [? feathers. ?] And they, they didn't know what to do. They want to be with us all day when I'm not working. And finally, came to, to talk that I would like to go my own business. When my boss came back from the, the operation, he was working already.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he said, if you want to go-- he knew that I want to learn the business. And, and this gave me a more chance. This was before Passover, Easter. Were you-- did you observe the Jewish holidays when you were in American?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. Before Passover, and the salesman comes in. And he wants  an order. And I didn't know what to order.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, do you know about? You give me 20% more than you send the last year-- the same order, what what you sent last year, if on everything, give me 20% more. Because I had more business than the boss had by himself, because they-- I was so nice to them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the customers, they like me. They told other people. And they only came to me to buy of me more stuff.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the man says he'll give you money to buy your own business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Listen to this. He said, look-- my cousin, to me-- I will give you the money. Put it in the bank for you-- under your name. But don't go yet in the business, because you're too young to go in that business. You have to learn more. I said to him, look, if you said you a Carmel and if you said your word that you're going to give me, I'm 100% sure you're going to give me. I don't want the money unless you're going to allow me to look for a business. Then I take the money. But before you not allowing me, I don't want the money. You keep the money, I said to him. I trust you more than you think. And he said, he talking like this. I will give you the money and look for a business. But make sure that you know what to do. I said, I will know what to do. And he gave me $4,200 in that time-- $4,200 and go in business. It was a lot of money. But, still, I couldn't buy a decent business. I had to buy it with merchandise, you know, and the business. So I boughted a store. But if you didn't order the-- a bread or a milk, they didn't have for you. You had to order day before. Then they had the bread for you or the milk for you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this kind of business, she, she couldn't-- she was running the business. The husband was a nothing, you know? But he-- she was running the business.

speaker: interviewee
text: I bought that business. I took apart everything. I built my own shelves. I built everything. I took in a new refrigerator. I start-- and, and that salesman came to me to sell me the-- but want to lend me money. He came to me for an order. He came to me. And I gave him an order. I gave him an order for $1,500. And I wa-- I'm ready to open the store. But I didn't have the merchandise yet. I called up the company. And I said, I gave you an order, gave you to sale-- to that salesman an order. And I didn't get the merchandise. I'm ready to open. I cannot open without the merchandise. And I prepared that time a lot of sugar, because I want to give away the sugar with low cost price in order the customer should come in for the sugar. But they have to buy $5 minimum with that bag sugar. And I prepared a lot of sugar to give away, below cost price. And then start the Korean War. This was in 1951. The Korean War started. And I-- the people coming in-- yeah. And I did-- they took again the same order. And they send me the order. But I order it again. And they send me the first order. Two orders, they send it to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I accepted both orders. I saw what's going on, the people buying everything. So I am going to sell both orders. So I accepted both orders. Then the, the, the, the manager from that place, he said to that salesmen, he owe-- we didn't get any money from them, from him. And he has already over $3,000 merchandise from us. He owes us that much money. So he came to me. And he said-- but the manager told him I should give him pay him at least for one order. I said, look, I will pay you for both orders. But give me 5% off. Let's go a little bit back to-- because what you're telling us is how, even as a little boy, you knew how to survive. And now you're telling us how you survived in business in United States with your wife and your family. So you stayed New York then, for how long now?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- till 1967, I was staying in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just you, your wife, and your daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my, my, my daughter passed away 1963.

speaker: interviewer
text: She died of what, Sam?

speaker: interviewee
text: She-- colitis.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was she?

speaker: interviewee
text: 16 And a half years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she was your only child?

speaker: interviewee
text: That time, she was-- no, I had already my son.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was your son born, Sam?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 19-- he is now 38, 38. And I'm 78. It's 40 years back.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's your son's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Joseph.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was born in New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was born in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, so you and your wife now have two children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Two children.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're in New York.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're in the business.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened? Then something-- did you make a switch and move?

speaker: interviewee
text: Listen to this. And I build up that business. It was a, a very good business. I made money, a lot of money, in that business. Then the neighborhood change. Puerto Ricans came into that neighborhood. And they didn't know for going into the store to buy. They know only to come in to take whatever they wanted and walk out with it. They didn't want to just buy. And the, the old Jewish people, mostly my cousins, was moved away from there. And I see it's going down. I, I cannot stay. A, a, a young boy came in. He goes over to the shelves and takes merchandise and, and walks out with it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you decide to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Listen to this. So I grabbed him. And my wife starts yelling at me, let him go! Let him go! Don't touch him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he grabbed out a knife, and you know? And that's why she start yelling at me I should let, let him go with the merchandise. And I let him go.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I saw that I cannot stay anymore, then I found a supermarket in Sheepshead Bay. There was a nice neighborhood. And, And, I was working. I was making good. The my wife-- yeah, my daughter passed away. My daughter passed away. My wife got sick, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did she get sick with, Sam?

speaker: interviewee
text: She got that time a-- she has arthritis on the spine. And she couldn't walk down from the bed. And sure enough, it was that bad that I had a woman taking care on her and the children. And I saw that they're getting worse and worse. I went in to the doctor. And we had a private doctor. And I said to him, look, I will give you all the money I have. I don't care for money. I'm young. I'm going to make other money. You save her life. I want her to be alive. I want her be healthy. Do whatever you have to do. He said, I'm sorry. I cannot take your money. And I cannot have any medicine for arthritis. He said, you change the climate. Then this might help her getting well. And then what I decided, I'm going to go to California, Miami, and back to New York. I made a, a, a tickets like this and to go around on the, on the airplane. I came in February to Los Angeles. The sun is, is shining.

speaker: interviewer
text: The, the flowers are blooming. Everything is blooming. I don't need even my jacket to go out. This is heaven, not, not-- just in February?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I called my wife. And I told her about it, that in Los Angeles, it's this kind of weather. And you're going to be in Los Angeles, the way I see it. But let me [? have your time. ?] She said, I would fly now already, if I could. I said, we'll take a little time. But you're going to-- sooner or later, you going to be here. Because I like it for you to be healthy. Anyway, I had the ticket to Miami. I went to Miami. [INAUDIBLE] lived in Miami already. And I got in touch with him. And he said, I'm going to do something for you what even a father is not doing. I'm going to buy a big hotel. We going to be partners. You going to run it. You going to have help. And you gonna-- you gonna pay off me my part. You going to remain with that then. Then at night came down rain, so heavy rain. Here, it was nice, beautiful. And here come the rain. And I said, no, Miami is not for me. And I said to my cousin, I thank you very much. You're very fine from your side that you want to help me like that. But I cannot accept it. I'm not changing this climate because I didn't make no living. I can always make a living. I'm sure of myself. And-- OK.
